article.title: Renault to Deploy 350 Wandercraft Calvin Humanoid Robots Across French EV Factories in 18-Month Industrial Push
article.summary: Renault Group plans to roll out 350 Calvin-40 humanoid robots built by Wandercraft across its manufacturing operations within 18 months, starting with tire handling and body-shop logistics at the Douai ElectriCity plant in northern France.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Renault Group has announced plans to deploy 350 humanoid robots across its manufacturing facilities over the next 18 months, in what amounts to one of the largest confirmed industrial rollouts of bipedal robots by any automaker to date. The initiative centers on the Calvin-40, a two-legged robot developed by New York-based startup Wandercraft, and was unveiled during Renault's "futuREady" strategic plan event on March 10, 2026.

The first Calvin-40 units are already operational at Renault's Douai ElectriCity plant in northern France, where they handle tire placement and body-shop logistics on production lines assembling the Renault 5 electric vehicle.

## What We Know

**The Calvin-40 robot**

The Calvin-40 is a headless, bipedal humanoid robot capable of lifting up to 40 kilograms (approximately 90 pounds), according to [InsideEVs](https://insideevs.com/news/790259/renault-wandercraft-humanoid-robot-ev-factory/). Instead of a head, the machine relies on waist-mounted video cameras and LED status lights for perception and communication. Wandercraft developed the current second-generation model in just 40 days using AI-accelerated training methods, and the robot's walking speed has doubled over the past six months compared to its April 2025 predecessor.

The design deliberately sacrifices the dexterity and visual sophistication of more headline-grabbing humanoids in favor of payload capacity and reliability in repetitive industrial tasks. As [Interesting Engineering](https://interestingengineering.com/ai-robotics/france-renault-350-humanoid-robot-factory) noted, the robots are engineered for "brownfield" deployment, meaning they can operate within existing factory layouts without requiring infrastructure modifications.

**Current factory operations**

At the Douai ElectriCity complex, Calvin-40 units are performing two primary tasks: placing tires onto conveyor belts feeding the final assembly line, and carrying panels through the body shop. Both are physically demanding jobs that involve repetitive heavy lifting hundreds of times per shift. [InsideEVs](https://insideevs.com/news/790259/renault-wandercraft-humanoid-robot-ev-factory/) reported that the ElectriCity facility currently assembles electric vehicles including the Megane E-Tech sedan and Scenic E-Tech SUV, alongside the Renault 5 EV.

The deployment targets tasks described by [Interesting Engineering](https://interestingengineering.com/ai-robotics/france-renault-350-humanoid-robot-factory) as "repetitive or ergonomically challenging" operations that are difficult to automate with conventional fixed robotic arms due to the need for mobility across open factory floors.

**Investment and partnership**

Renault invested $75 million in June 2025 to acquire a minority stake in Wandercraft, according to [InsideEVs](https://insideevs.com/news/790259/renault-wandercraft-humanoid-robot-ev-factory/). The investment was intended to accelerate robot development for Renault's specific automotive manufacturing requirements. Wandercraft, which originally developed exoskeleton technology for rehabilitation, pivoted the Calvin platform toward industrial applications after recognizing demand from the automotive sector.

**Production efficiency targets**

Renault has set ambitious targets tied to the humanoid deployment: the company aims to cut production hours per vehicle by 30 percent and reduce overall production costs by 20 percent over five years, as reported by [InsideEVs](https://insideevs.com/news/790259/renault-wandercraft-humanoid-robot-ev-factory/). Reaching those targets will require scaling from the current handful of operational units to the full fleet of 350 robots across multiple facilities.

## What We Don't Know

Renault has not disclosed which factories beyond Douai will receive Calvin-40 deployments, or how the 350 units will be distributed across its manufacturing network. The company has also not specified the per-unit cost of the robots or the total investment beyond the initial $75 million Wandercraft stake.

The Calvin-40's current limitations in speed and dexterity mean it cannot yet perform precision final-assembly work. Whether future iterations will close that gap, or whether Renault plans to pair the humanoids with other automation technologies for more complex tasks, remains unclear.

It is also uncertain how the deployment will affect workforce levels at Renault's plants. The company has framed the initiative as relieving workers from physically demanding duties rather than replacing jobs, but has not provided specifics on headcount plans.

## Why It Matters

The Renault-Wandercraft rollout represents a significant departure from the pattern that has defined humanoid robotics in manufacturing to date, where most deployments have remained at the pilot or demonstration stage. By committing to 350 units within 18 months, Renault is moving directly from prototype to production-scale adoption.

The approach also contrasts with higher-profile humanoid programs. Tesla's Optimus and Figure's 02 have attracted considerable attention, but neither has announced a comparable confirmed order from an outside manufacturer. Renault's strategy of deploying a simpler, purpose-built robot for material handling rather than waiting for a general-purpose humanoid to mature could prove to be a more pragmatic path to near-term returns on investment.

For the broader automotive industry, the deployment will serve as a closely watched test case. If Renault achieves measurable gains in production efficiency and worker ergonomics, other manufacturers are likely to accelerate their own humanoid adoption timelines. If the robots struggle to scale beyond controlled pilot environments, it may reinforce skepticism about whether bipedal form factors offer genuine advantages over wheeled or tracked alternatives in factory settings.
